displayTitle: 2025-09-09 - Māori Affairs Committee - Briefing on Treaty settlement legislation and Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, How public organisations are fulfilling Treaty settlemen
topic: Briefing on Treaty settlement legislation and Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, How public organisations are fulfilling Treaty settlemen
description: Wednesday 10 September 2025 | Māori Affairs Committee Briefing on Treaty settlement legislation and Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, How public organisations are fulfilling Treaty settlements Hearing of evidence Submitter list: Department of Conservation - Sheridan Smith, Deputy Director-General Treaty Relationships - Peter Galvin, Acting Deputy Director-General Policy - Huia Forbes, Kaihautu Treaty Relationships Advice and Oversight and Acting Director Treaty Negotiations and Land - Jos Fryer, Principal Treaty Relationships Advisor
descriptionSummary: Briefing on Treaty settlement legislation and Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, How public organisations are fulfilling Treaty settlemen. chunks.text: Important, uh, for us as a committee, and I'm sure for the crown itself, is that we're doing everything we can to make sure we meet the commitments that we've signed ourselves up to in law with regards to treaty settlements. Um, so with that, um, obviously the document that, that we have as a primary focus is obviously the, uh, audit, the general's report, and it's got a number of recommendations, uh, within that.

But perhaps to start this, this hearing, and I, I want this to be more of a discussion that's, I think that's the most helpful one of us getting to the best outcome, um, with regards to this. But you can, how about when you spend a bit of time opening, uh, with some, some commentary about, uh, where you see the organized organization's at, it's your successes and your challenges itself.
So, no.

Um, thank you for your invitation, uh, for to appear before you this morning, um, to respond to the treaty settlement commitments, uh, report and recommendations. We do have a sort of a, some opening comments and, uh, that will probably take 10 to 15 minutes on.

Okay. Um, so I'm gonna start with some introductory comments. Uh, this is a crucial in central COPA for TE Dock, uh, as the holder of the largest number of treaty settlement commitments. And we remain committed to working with our treaty partners and continuing to improve our implementation, our partnerships, and our relationships with Iwi and Hapu and PSGs across the MuTu, uh, course Sharon and Smith.

um, I have been in this role for five weeks, uh, and so I'm grateful to be here, but I'm grateful, right. Uh, we manage the public conservation estate on behalf of the Crown and all New Zealanders, and that is about a third of the land area of RO in New Zealand.

Uh, as you would expect, land and UA is at the heart of breaches and treaty settlements, and it is natural that we as the key agency managing land on behalf of the Crown hold a large portfolio of the settlement commitments. Our commitment portfolio is complex.

It comprises both one-off as well as enduring commitments, uh, cultural and relationship redress, financial and commercial redress regulatory commitments, and some custom redress as well. Uh, we recognize that our ability to, uh, deliver the redress is affected by a number of issues, and this includes our own capacity and capability, the readiness of treaty partners to receive redress or trigger it, um, and treaty partners competing priorities.

We are not the only game in town, um, and delivery issues may affect a treaty partners capacity, and we are keen to be, to understand those delivery issues. Mm-hmm. Uh, and across the border public sector that may be affecting, uh, our partners and their ability to engage.

And that relates to one of the O'S recommendations about the system, uh, on slide three, which is the infographic. Um, so this summarizes our various commitment types and the relative status. Uh, and this is an example of the enhanced reporting the OAG has recommended, and it details the types of commitments we have.

Um, so overall, we hold nearly three and a half thousand commitments. 33% of these are complete, and a further 50%, 57% are on track. We do have eight commitments with a status of delivery issues, uh, which is about 0.2%. Um, the balance of our commitments are either not yet triggered, for example, rights of first refusal or not yet entered into the database.

Yeah, Thank you.

And we prioritize, uh, making sure that those are done. But we or Peter some examples. Oh, yeah.
